title: At Home
description: Ammini misses school, and her brother Unni misses his aunt. Both of them miss playing in the park. But everyone has to stay indoors. Maybe they can go out for a walk today? Ammini hopes so. A day in the life of two children in a time of lockdowns and social distancing.

pages.text:
Coor, coor, cooor...
Ammini rubs the sleep out of her eyes
as she wakes up to the call of the
chembothu nesting in the mango tree.

“I didn’t keep my school uniform ready last night,” grumbles
Ammini. “Oh, I forgot, I don’t have to go to school.”
Achan is making coffee and idlis in the kitchen. Ammini runs
downstairs and gives him a good-morning hug. She hopes that
the family can go out for a walk today.

“The idlis are so good, Achan!” says Ammini.
Her four-year-old brother Unni walks into the kitchen.
“Kamala cheriyamma?” he calls out, sleepily.
Ammini and Unni’s aunt is his favourite playmate.

“She is still at the hospital, Unni. She said
that she would come home as soon as the sick
people get better,” says Achan.
Unni’s face crumples. Kamala cheriyamma is a
nurse, and has not come home for weeks now.

“Come, Unni. Let’s see the flowers that have
bloomed in the garden,” says Ammini.

“Look, there is the arali. See the yellow in the middle?
The honeybees will come to carry that away. And look
at the techi, see the big bunches? My teacher says it is
called jungle flame in English.”

“Hello!”
“Abu uncle, I like your mask,”
Ammini says.
Abu uncle salutes, and asks,
“What are both of you doing?”
“Looking at the flowers.
Where is the police car?” Ammini asks.

“Still asleep after screaming with her siren all
night long. Stay at home, otherwise she will
wake up and say, weeeewoooweeeewooo,” says
Abu uncle. Abu uncle waves and walks towards
his mother Zeenat aunty’s house.
It is almost time for Eid. Every year, Zeenat
aunty makes delicious biryani and sheer korma.
Will they be able to go for the feast this year,
Ammini wonders.

“Ammini, time for school,” Amma says.
“Good morning, Maya teacher!”
Ammini’s classmates are waving at each
other from the tiny squares on the laptop.
School is so different now. Ammini’s best
friend Nina is absent today. She shares
the computer with her big brother, so
she attends school twice a week.

Ammini misses Nina, but she misses
school even more. She misses the
bumpy rides to school in Gigi chettan’s
rickshaw. She misses splashing in the
puddles on the playground with Nina.
She misses whispering and exchanging
tiffin boxes at lunchtime. And her
favourite set of colour pencils is still in
school. When will she ever get it back?

School ends at lunchtime. “Chechi, can we go
to the playground?” Unni asks Ammini.
Ammini looks at Amma, who shakes her head.
“Not today, Unni. But we can play the volcano
lava game Amma showed us!” Ammini says.

Ammini and Unni race through the house
gathering up cushions, pillows and blankets.
They take their toy animals and arrange
them around the room.
“Come on, Unni, we have to rescue the
animals. And remember, the floor is lava!”

“That was so much fun!” Ammini says as she
and Unni lie on the blanket with their toys.
“Yes, but I miss the slides and swings and
grass, chechi.”

“Me too,” Ammini says. “I also miss the beach
and the cotton candy and the balloons and
going to Ammumma and Appoopan.”
They imagine going for walks, running in the
playground, hugging their grandparents tight.

The sun is an orange marble now.
“Time for a walk, maybe,” Ammini thinks.
It has been a while since she last put on her shoes. Amma
and Achan are sitting on the sofa, listening to music.
Ammini takes Unni’s hand and they run to squeeze in
between Amma and Achan.
“I hope we can go for a walk soon or to the beach. But until
then, I’m glad that I can spend so much time with Unni,
Achan and Amma,” Ammini thinks.
